ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tea food product class; no specific Pu-erh subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers tea infusions broadly; no Pu-erh-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 covers tea as a substance; no Pu-erh-specific code exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of brewed Pu-erh tea, approximately 240 ml, served hot or warm as is typical in Chinese and Malaysian Chinese restaurant settings.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, hot, leaf, brewed — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 14355)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for brewed black tea used as the closest approved analogue for brewed Pu-erh tea. Pu-erh is a post-fermented tea made from Camellia sinensis; its brewed macronutrient profile is essentially identical to other brewed teas (near-zero calories, protein, fat, and carbohydrates per 100 ml). Values adapted to per-100g basis for a brewed beverage. IMR Malaysia MyFCD and HPB Singapore Foodo do not carry a specific Pu-erh tea entry; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved source with a defensible match.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Beverages — Plain brewed tea (no sugar added)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed tea (unsweetened) is not assigned a formal GI value in the University of Sydney GI Database because it contains negligible glycemic carbohydrates. By convention for essentially zero-carbohydrate beverages, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value), gi_category is 'low', and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. This is consistent with peer-reviewed literature confirming that unsweetened tea does not raise blood glucose. Pu-erh tea served without sugar or milk contains no glycemic carbohydrates.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium content; potential vasodilatory polyphenols
hypertension.guidance: Unsweetened Pu-erh tea contains approximately 3 mg sodium per 100 ml and no added salt, making it a hypertension-friendly beverage; however, individuals should avoid adding salt, high-sodium broths, or excessive sugar, and should note that caffeine may transiently raise blood pressure in sensitive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate and glycemic load; bioactive polyphenols may support insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Unsweetened Pu-erh tea has a glycemic load of effectively zero and does not raise postprandial blood glucose; epidemiological and in vitro evidence suggests its theabrownins and gallic acid may support insulin sensitivity, making it a suitable beverage for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat; polyphenol antioxidants; potential LDL-lowering effect
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pu-erh tea contains no saturated fat or cholesterol and provides polyphenolic antioxidants; clinical studies suggest regular consumption of fermented Pu-erh tea may modestly reduce LDL cholesterol and triglycerides, supporting cardiovascular health when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pu-erh tea (普洱茶, Pǔ'ěr chá) is a post-fermented, aged tea originating from Yunnan Province, China, produced from the large-leaf variety of Camellia sinensis. Unlike green or oolong teas, Pu-erh undergoes a microbial fermentation process — either naturally over years (raw/sheng Pu-erh) or accelerated through pile-fermentation (ripe/shou Pu-erh) — which gives it a distinctive earthy, mellow, and sometimes woody or mushroom-like flavour profile with a smooth, full-bodied mouthfeel. The brewed liquor ranges from deep amber to dark reddish-brown. In Singapore and Malaysia, Pu-erh tea is commonly served at Chinese seafood restaurants, zi char establishments, and dim sum venues as a complimentary or low-cost beverage, often poured from large teapots throughout the meal. Nutritionally, brewed Pu-erh tea is essentially calorie-free (approximately 1 kcal per 100 ml), with negligible protein, fat, and carbohydrate content, making it suitable for virtually all dietary patterns. It contains caffeine (approximately 30–70 mg per cup depending on steeping time and grade) and a range of bioactive polyphenols including theabrownins, gallic acid, and catechin derivatives formed during fermentation. These compounds have been associated in peer-reviewed literature with potential benefits including modest LDL cholesterol reduction, improved insulin sensitivity, and antioxidant activity. The glycemic load is effectively zero, making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. Sodium content is negligible (~3 mg per 100 ml), supporting its suitability for hypertension management. Pu-erh tea is vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. It is typically served hot or warm, without milk or sugar in traditional Chinese settings, though sweetened or milk-added variants exist.
common_user_queries: Is Pu-erh tea good for diabetes?
How many calories in Pu-erh tea?
What is the glycemic index of Pu-erh tea?

What is the difference between raw and ripe Pu-erh?
Can I drink Pu-erh tea if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No specific Pu-erh tea entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy brewed black tea (NDB No. 14355), which is the closest approved analogue. The macronutrient profile of brewed Pu-erh tea is essentially identical to other brewed teas (near-zero energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate). GI is assigned by convention as 1 (sentinel for effectively zero) since no formal GI test exists for Pu-erh tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. protein_g is naturally zero — brewed Pu-erh tea contains no measurable protein per USDA SR Legacy brewed tea data. 2. fat_g is naturally zero — brewed Pu-erh tea contains no fat by nature. 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are naturally zero for a plain brewed tea beverage. 4. sugar_g is naturally zero — no added sugar; trace carbohydrate (0.3 g/100 ml) is from tea solids, not free sugars. 5. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero — brewed tea liquid contains no dietary fibre. 6. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero — plant-derived beverage. 7. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 confirmed. 8. Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy brewed black tea (NDB No. 14355) as the closest approved analogue; no specific Pu-erh entry in HPB, MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. 9. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type throughout as no Pu-erh-specific codes exist in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT. 10. The food is listed under Malaysian cuisine in the field survey source but Pu-erh tea is of Chinese origin (Yunnan Province); region_of_origin reflects true origin. 11. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg, calcium_mg are reported as 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy brewed tea data (confirmed zero or below detection threshold). 12. Serving size of 240 g reflects a standard cup of brewed tea as a beverage.
